title: Use commander, not citty, for the burrow CLI
rationale: SPEC.md §6 lists citty as the CLI framework choice, but every os-eco sibling tool (mulch, seeds, canopy) uses commander, and burrow's package.json was scaffolded with commander. Switching to citty would diverge from the ecosystem with no upside; the SPEC line is outdated, not load-bearing. Cross-tool consistency is the more important constraint.

content: Public types live in src/core/types.ts (per SPEC §7 layout), not src/types.ts. Domain-internal types co-locate with their module (e.g. src/provider/types.ts, src/db/schema.ts). The CLAUDE.md mention of 'src/types.ts' is generic scaffold prose; SPEC.md and the existing tree are the source of truth.

burrow CLI commands open the DB via openDatabase({path: resolvePaths().dbPath}) and wrap their action in a try/finally that calls db.close(). Use type-safe option spreading when forwarding optional fields (e.g. {...(priority !== undefined ? {priority} : {})}) so noUncheckedIndexedAccess + exactOptionalPropertyTypes don't reject the call.

burrow CLI long-running commands (logs --follow, events --follow) wire SIGINT/SIGTERM → AbortController.abort() in main.ts and pass signal into runXxxCommand. The polling generator returns when the signal fires, so the command returns its summary without leaking a timer or an open db handle. Pattern: const ac = new AbortController(); const onSig = () => ac.abort(); process.on('SIGINT', onSig); process.on('SIGTERM', onSig); try { ... } finally { process.off('SIGINT', onSig); process.off('SIGTERM', onSig); db.close(); }

burrow CLI tail commands (logs, events) auto-pick output mode: NDJSON when --json is set OR stdout is not a TTY; pretty single-line otherwise. Tests pass isTty:false to force NDJSON deterministically. resolveJsonMode in cli/commands/{logs,events}.ts encodes this — duplicate intentionally rather than hoist, since logs and events have independent option shapes today.

name: client-per-cli-invocation
description: burrow CLI commands open a fresh Client.open() per invocation (no daemon) via withClient(). Long-running commands (logs/events --follow, chat) wire SIGINT/SIGTERM to AbortController and drop the listeners in finally{} so process.exit doesn't leak signal handlers. The Client owns DB + EventBus + AgentRegistry + Logger lifecycle; close() tears down both bus and DB.

content: CLI exit codes follow SPEC §16: 0 success, 1 generic, 2 NotFoundError, 3 ValidationError, 4 SandboxError (BurrowError subclasses). exitCodeFor(err) in src/cli/main.ts maps the BurrowError hierarchy to the right code; per-id batch commands (stop, destroy) call process.exit(1) when any outcome.ok is false.

name: burrow-id-driven-workspace-paths
description: burrow up/fork generate the burrow id (via generateId('burrow')) before materializing the workspace, then pass id to BurrowsRepo.create so the on-disk workspace dir embeds the same id (projectsDir/<slug>/workspaces/<bur-id>). This keeps the row id and workspace path one-to-one for destroy → removeMaterializedWorkspace later.

content: burrow destroy stores the recovered MaterializedWorkspaceSource in burrow.providerStateJson.workspaceSource at up/fork time so destroy can reverse via removeMaterializedWorkspace without re-deriving worktree vs clone. Workspace removal failures are caught and recorded (workspaceRemoved=false) but never abort archival — the row is still marked destroyed; user can manually remove the dir.

name: client-namespace-wrapper
description: Client class (src/lib/client.ts) wraps the five SPEC §15 namespaces (burrows/runs/inbox/events/agents) as instance fields, each a thin wrapper over the underlying primitive (BurrowsRepo, Inbox, EventBus, AgentRegistry). Sub-classes expose .raw / .rawBus accessors for callers that need the lower-level primitive (e.g. send/chat use client.agents.raw to get the AgentRegistry for isSpawnPerTurn checks).

content: burrow doctor accepts --project <root> (defaults to cwd) for project-scoped checks and --no-project to opt out; assertDoctorOk(report) is the helper burrow up uses to gate sandbox launch on toolchain + burrow.toml health (SPEC §19). Checks have status 'ok' | 'fail' | 'warn'; report.ok = no 'fail' rows (warns are informational, e.g. burrow.toml absent).

name: burrow-up-toml-pipeline
description: burrow up pipeline (Phase 8): (1) loadBurrowToml(projectRoot) → ValidationError on parse fail, null on absent; (2) resolve [sandbox].network with CLI > burrow.toml > 'none' default; (3) runDoctor + assertDoctorOk before any side effects (so a missing toolchain refuses up rather than orphaning a worktree); (4) loadSecretStore + resolveEnv to populate profile.setEnv; (5) materialize workspace; (6) BurrowsRepo.create. Default branch falls back to burrow.toml [project].default_branch; project name falls back to [project].name. Test seams: skipDoctor, doctorRunner, opResolver, hostEnv, envOverrides.

name: agent-toml-shortcut
description: Two ergonomic surfaces wire built-in agents into burrow.toml without manual edits: (1) 'bw init <agent>...' bakes [[agents]] stanzas during scaffolding; (2) 'bw agents add <id>' appends a stanza to an existing file (idempotent — re-adding a declared id no-ops with a reason). Both share renderAgentStanza in src/cli/commands/agents-add.ts and resolveAgentAlias in src/runtime/aliases.ts (claude→claude-code, cc→claude-code, sp→sapling, cx→codex). agents-add re-parses the mutated file via parseBurrowTomlOrThrow before writing — defensive belt-and-suspenders against malformed appends.

name: synchronous-cli-prompt-dispatcher
description: burrow prompt drives a single run end-to-end inside the CLI process: enqueue+claim run, claim pending steering messages, prepareWorkspace, buildSpawnCommand, runSandboxed, then drain stdout/stderr line-by-line through runtime.parseEvents into appendAndPublish. Bypasses RunLoop (which is for daemonized N-burrow callers). Promise.all over [proc.exited, consumeStdout, consumeStderr] keeps the child from stalling on backpressure; .catch on each consumer captures stream errors so finalize still runs. Codex is special-cased: writeCodexPromptFile(workspace, run.id, composeCodexPrompt(prompt, msgs)) before spawn, since the runtime contract doesn't expose the prompt to prepareWorkspace.

content: bw prompt's default agent resolution: --agent <id> overrides; otherwise loadBurrowToml(burrow.projectRoot) and pick config.agents[0].id; otherwise ValidationError suggesting --agent or 'bw agents add'. The loader is injected as a test seam (burrowTomlLoader).

CLI exit codes (per SPEC §16) extended in src/cli/main.ts exitCodeFor(): AgentNotInstalled and AgentRuntimeError both map to 4 (runtime/sandbox error). bw prompt explicitly exits 4 on run.state==='failed' and 1 on 'cancelled'.

name: per-run-proxy-lifecycle
description: When SandboxProfile.network === 'restricted', burrow prompt starts a per-run loopback proxy (startProxy) before runSandboxed and stops it in the finally that wraps proc.exited. The runtime profile is a shallow clone with proxyAddress filled in (transient, never persisted on burrow.profileJson). The runtime command's env carries HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/http_proxy/https_proxy/NO_PROXY=. Spawn rejection also stops the proxy. Test seam: input.startProxy lets prompt.test stub the handle.

Commander.js .option(name, desc, default) sets the option to the literal default string when the user omits the flag. For --network in src/cli/main.ts the default was 'none', so opts.network was always 'none' — collapseUpOptions then forwarded it, and resolveNetworkPolicy in up.ts saw 'flag !== undefined' and ignored burrow.toml [sandbox].network entirely. Symptom: 'bw up' on a project whose burrow.toml declares network="restricted" still wrote profile.network="none" to the DB, masking the burrow-14b6 proxy fix end-to-end (no proxy ever started since the DB said 'none').
resolution: Don't pass a default to Commander when burrow.toml is the intended source of truth — leave the third arg off so opts.network stays undefined when the flag is omitted, and resolveNetworkPolicy falls through to config.sandbox.network. Help text now explains the resolution order ('defaults to burrow.toml, then none').

name: completions-walk-program-tree
description: burrow completions <shell> renders bash/zsh/fish from the live commander Command tree. Walks program.commands once, skipping the completions subcommand itself; emits one script that registers under both 'burrow' and 'bw' bin names (COMPLETION_BINARIES). Adding a new top-level command means the next regenerated script picks it up automatically — no manual completion table to maintain. Uses optionInfo to flatten short/long flag pairs and escapeShellSingle for description quoting.

content: burrow upgrade prints the upgrade command rather than executing it (SPEC §16.1). bun-first hint with npm fallback, --json shape '{command, current}'. The CLI never writes to the user's global bin dir itself — telling a user how to upgrade is safe; running 'bun install -g' under their fingers is not.

Recovery hints are wired at the *deepest stable throw site* — e.g. BurrowsRepo.require() carries the 'run `burrow list` to see known ids' hint, so every CLI surface that resolves a burrow id inherits it without each command repeating the hint. Same idea for inbox.send (empty body), declarative.ts (missing settings template path), and prompt.ts (--metadata parse). Add new hints at the throw, not in the command wrapper.
